segments.text: This video highlights the new security
report window in Sonar Cube for Visual
Studio, displaying security hotspots,
vulnerabilities, and dependency risks
found by Sona Cube. It's designed to
help you better visualize and zero in on
its security findings. You'll need to be
in connected mode to Sonic Cube cloud or
server. Additionally, to see dependency
risks, you will also need an advanced
security subscription. There are links
in the description to help with both of
these. Okay, I'm in Visual Studio and
I'm already in connected mode with my
project bound to a Sona Cube server
project. At the bottom, you can see the
standard error pane open. To see the new
security report, go to extensions and
select Sona Cube connected mode view
security report. And this opens up the
security report pane. By default, it
will display all the security issues
that have been surfaced for this
project. If you click on an issue, it
will open up the Sonic Cube rule help
panel where you can understand more
about why this is an issue, how to fix
it, and its potential impact. Plus, you
can also visualize the flow for that
issue.
You are able to view security issues in
the open documents or in the current
document.
And you can also filter by severity and
status.
For example, you may wish to only see
blockers which have been resolved in
this example.
To view dependency risks, assuming you
have an advanced security subscription,
just click on the tab and it will
highlight these. From here, you can
manage them. For example, you can choose
to change the status and add a suitable
comment that will be visible to your
team in Sona Cube.
You can also choose to dig deeper by
opening the issue in Sona Cube where you
can get the detailed description around
the risk as well as guidance on how to
resolve it. Sonar also takes a proactive
approach to improving open-source
security by paying maintainers to follow
and document secure practices. You can
see their insights here.
And that's it. You can find all of your
security issues, including any
dependency risks, in one place, right
where you code in Visual Studio. For
more details, check out the Sonic Cube
documentation linked below. Happy
coding.

fullText: This video highlights the new security report window in Sonar Cube for Visual Studio, displaying security hotspots, vulnerabilities, and dependency risks found by Sona Cube. It's designed to help you better visualize and zero in on its security findings. You'll need to be in connected mode to Sonic Cube cloud or server. Additionally, to see dependency risks, you will also need an advanced security subscription. There are links in the description to help with both of these. Okay, I'm in Visual Studio and I'm already in connected mode with my project bound to a Sona Cube server project. At the bottom, you can see the standard error pane open. To see the new security report, go to extensions and select Sona Cube connected mode view security report. And this opens up the security report pane. By default, it will display all the security issues that have been surfaced for this project. If you click on an issue, it will open up the Sonic Cube rule help panel where you can understand more about why this is an issue, how to fix it, and its potential impact. Plus, you can also visualize the flow for that issue. You are able to view security issues in the open documents or in the current document. And you can also filter by severity and status. For example, you may wish to only see blockers which have been resolved in this example. To view dependency risks, assuming you have an advanced security subscription, just click on the tab and it will highlight these. From here, you can manage them. For example, you can choose to change the status and add a suitable comment that will be visible to your team in Sona Cube. You can also choose to dig deeper by opening the issue in Sona Cube where you can get the detailed description around the risk as well as guidance on how to resolve it. Sonar also takes a proactive approach to improving open-source security by paying maintainers to follow and document secure practices. You can see their insights here. And that's it. You can find all of your security issues, including any dependency risks, in one place, right where you code in Visual Studio. For more details, check out the Sonic Cube documentation linked below. Happy coding.
